Stunning -- deslumbrante / lindíssimo(a)
"The cafe had a stunning view of the sea."
Cozy -- aconchegante
"It was a small, cozy cafe."
Rooftop -- terraço / topo do prédio
"We had dinner at a rooftop restaurant."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its meaning in Portuguese.

Tall
Curly
Friendly
Narrow
Crowded
Peaceful
Ancient
Stunning
Cozy
Rooftop

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Read the description and answer the questions.

On her last trip, Tânia visited a lovely small ancient town by the sea. The streets were narrow and quiet, and the old houses were beautiful. There she met a tall, friendly man with short curly hair. He worked at a cozy little cafe with a stunning rooftop view. In the morning the square was peaceful, but in the evening it became crowded and full of music. It is now her favorite place.

1. What kind of town did Tânia visit?
A A lovely small ancient town by the sea.
B A big modern city.
C A new town in the mountains.
2. What did the man look like?
A Short with long straight hair.
B Tall and friendly, with short curly hair.
C Old and quiet, with grey hair.
3. How was the square in the evening?
A Peaceful and empty.
B Crowded and full of music.
C Closed and dark.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Adjective Order

Grammar

Quando você usa dois ou três adjetivos, em inglês eles seguem uma ordem natural: opinião > tamanho > idade > cor + substantivo. Por isso dizemos "a lovely small ancient town", não "an ancient small lovely town". Dois adjetivos já soam ótimos; três é o máximo para o A2. / When you use two or three adjectives, in English they follow a natural order: opinion > size > age > color + noun. So we say "a lovely small ancient town", not "an ancient small lovely town". Two adjectives already sound great; three is the maximum for A2.

Ordem / OrderEnglishPortuguês
Opinion (what you think)a lovely viewuma vista linda
Opinion + sizea tall, friendly manum homem alto e simpático
Opinion + size + agea lovely small ancient townuma cidadezinha antiga e charmosa
Opinion + age + colora beautiful old blue dooruma linda porta azul antiga
Size + shape (hair)short curly haircabelo curto e cacheado

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the right word. Tap Ouvir to hear the full sentence.

"He is a tall, man."
"She has short hair."
"The old streets are very ."
"In the evening the square was ."
"The cafe had a view."
"It is a lovely small town."

Stage 2: Order the Description

Order

Listen first, then put the description in order.

?After that, she met a tall, friendly man with short curly hair.
?Finally, she watched the sunset and decided it was her favorite place.
?First, Tânia arrived in a small, ancient town by the sea.
?Next, he showed her a cozy rooftop cafe with a stunning view.
?Then she walked through the narrow, quiet streets and looked at the old houses.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it. Keep the adjectives in the right order.

He is a tall, friendly man.
Ele é um homem alto e simpático.
It is a lovely small ancient town.
É uma cidadezinha antiga e charmosa.
The streets are narrow and quiet.
As ruas são estreitas e tranquilas.
The cafe had a stunning rooftop view.
O café tinha uma vista deslumbrante do terraço.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best response for each situation.

You want to describe a kind, tall person. How do you say it?
A "He is a tall, friendly man."
B "He is a friendly tall man."
C "He is a man tall friendly."
You want to describe an old, small, charming town.
A "an ancient small lovely town"
B "a lovely small ancient town"
C "a small ancient lovely town"
You want to say a place is calm and quiet, not full of people.
A "It is crowded and noisy."
B "It is peaceful and quiet."
C "It is narrow and tall."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a person you admire and a place you love. Describe what the person looks like and what they are like, and paint a picture of the place. Use two or three adjectives in the right order (opinion > size > age > color). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Aula 15

1He is a tall, friendly man.Ele é um homem alto e simpático.
2She has short curly hair.Ela tem cabelo curto e cacheado.
3It is a lovely small ancient town.É uma cidadezinha antiga e charmosa.
4The streets are narrow and quiet.As ruas são estreitas e tranquilas.
5The cafe had a stunning rooftop view.O café tinha uma vista deslumbrante do terraço.

IN CLASS — Select Lesson

01
Who Is Tânia?
Diagnostic + Personal Introduction — 30 slides
02
At the Airport
Navigating International Terminals — 37 slides
03
Hotel Check-In
Booking and Settling In — 37 slides
04
Getting Around
Directions and Transportation — 37 slides
05
At the Restaurant
Ordering Food and Handling the Bill — 37 slides
06
Shopping and Souvenirs
Prices, Sizes, and Bargaining — 37 slides
07
Cultural Conversations
Places, People, and Traditions — 37 slides
08
Emergency Situations
Health, Safety, and Asking for Help — 37 slides
09
Family Travel
Coordinating with Family — 37 slides
10
Putting It All Together
Review and Confidence — 37 slides
11
Small Talk
Question forms & tag questions — 28 slides
12
Sharing Opinions
Agreeing & disagreeing — 28 slides
13
Making Plans
Future with going to & will — 28 slides
14
Travel Stories
Past continuous (was/were + -ing) — 28 slides
15
Describing People & Places
Adjective order (opinion-size-age-color) — 28 slides

Materiais Complementares — Aula 1

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: apresentação pessoal, viagens e culturas. Vocabulário: travel, destination, passport, experience, culture, visit, country, language. Gramática: Present Simple.

Filme
The Best Exotic Marigold Hotel (2012) -- Primeiros 30 minutos

Nos primeiros 30 minutos, os personagens falam sobre seus destinos ("destination"), experiências de viagem ("travel", "experience") e diferenças culturais ("culture"). Preste atenção em como usam o Present Simple para descrever rotinas: "I live in...", "I work as...", "I love to travel" -- mesmas estruturas da aula!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada vez que ouvir "travel", "country", "visit" ou "experience".

Podcast
English Learning for Curious Minds -- Episode #291: "Why Do People Travel?"

Episódio de 15 minutos que explora por que as pessoas viajam para outros países ("countries") e o que buscam em novas culturas ("cultures"). Usa vocabulário direto da aula: "destination", "experience", "visit", "language". Ritmo calmo, ideal para A2.

Dica: ouça e tente responder em voz alta: "I travel because..." usando o Present Simple da aula.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Speak English with Vanessa -- "Tell Me About Yourself in English" (12 min)

Vanessa ensina como se apresentar falando sobre de onde você vem ("country"), que idiomas fala ("language") e o que gosta de fazer ("I love to travel", "I enjoy visiting new places"). Usa o Present Simple o tempo todo -- perfeito para praticar a gramática da aula!

Dica: pause o vídeo e pratique: "My name is Tânia. I live in... I love to travel to..."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 2

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: no aeroporto. Vocabulário: flight, boarding pass, gate, luggage, check-in, terminal, departure, arrival. Gramática: Can/Could para pedidos educados.

Filme
The Terminal (2004) -- Cena do Check-in (minutos 5-20)

Tom Hanks chega ao terminal e precisa fazer check-in, mostrar o passaporte e encontrar o gate. Preste atenção nas frases com "Can I...?" e "Could I see your...?" -- mesmas estruturas da aula! Vocabulário direto: "flight", "terminal", "boarding pass", "luggage", "departure".

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Cada vez que ouvir "Can I..." ou "Could you...", pause e repita.

Podcast
Travel English by EnglishClass101 -- Lesson 2: "Checking In at the Airport"

Episódio de 10 minutos focado em check-in no aeroporto. Ensina exatamente as frases da aula: "Could I check in for my flight?", "Where is gate B12?", "Can I see your boarding pass?" Nível A2, fala pausada e clara.

Dica: ouça e pratique responder: "My flight is to...", "Here is my boarding pass", "Where is the gate?"

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy -- "Airport English: 30 Essential Phrases" (15 min)

Lucy ensina 30 frases para o aeroporto, incluindo "Could I have a window seat?", "Where is the departure gate?", "I need to check my luggage" -- todo o vocabulário da aula (flight, gate, luggage, terminal, arrival, departure) aparece com exemplos reais e pronúncia clara.

Dica: escolha 5 frases do vídeo e pratique em voz alta usando "Can I...?" e "Could you...?"

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 3

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: check-in no hotel. Vocabulário: reservation, room, key, checkout, receptionist, elevator, breakfast, floor. Gramática: Would like para pedidos educados.

Filme
Lost in Translation (2003) -- Cenas do Hotel Park Hyatt (minutos 3-25)

Bill Murray faz check-in no hotel em Tóquio: fala com o receptionist, pede a room key, pergunta sobre o breakfast e pede informações sobre o elevator e o floor do quarto. Preste atenção em "I'd like a room...", "Would you like breakfast?" -- exatamente a gramática da aula!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada frase com "I'd like..." ou "Would you like...?" que ouvir.

Podcast
All Ears English -- Episode #1578: "Hotel Check-in: 10 Phrases You Need"

Episódio de 12 minutos com as 10 frases mais usadas no check-in de hotel. Cobre todo o vocabulário da aula: "I have a reservation", "Could I have the room key?", "What floor is my room on?", "What time is checkout?", "Where is the elevator?" Inglês americano claro, ritmo A2.

Dica: ouça e pratique transformar cada frase em pedido educado: "I'd like a room with breakfast, please."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy -- "Hotel English: Check-in, Requests & Complaints" (18 min)

Lucy simula um check-in completo: reservation, room key, floor, elevator, breakfast e checkout. Ensina a pedir com "I'd like...", "Would you like...?" e "Could I have...?" -- as mesmas estruturas de "Would Like" da aula. Seção de complaints também usa vocabulário do hotel (receptionist, room).

Dica: pause o vídeo e simule seu próprio check-in: "I have a reservation under Rosa. I'd like a room on the fourth floor, please."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 4

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: se locomover pela cidade. Vocabulário: direction, street, corner, block, subway, map, bridge, sidewalk. Gramática: Imperativo + Preposições de lugar.

Filme
Midnight in Paris (2011) -- Primeiros 20 minutos: caminhadas pelas ruas

Owen Wilson caminha pelas ruas ("streets") de Paris, cruza pontes ("bridges"), vira em esquinas ("corners") e pede direções ("directions") para locais. Preste atenção nas instruções que ele recebe: "Turn left at the corner", "Walk across the bridge", "Go straight two blocks" -- mesmas estruturas de imperativo + preposições da aula!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote cada instrução de direção: "Turn...", "Go...", "Walk..."

Podcast
6 Minute English -- BBC Learning English: "Getting Around Town" (6 min)

Episódio de 6 minutos sobre como se locomover na cidade. Usa o vocabulário da aula: "subway", "street", "block", "sidewalk", "map". Apresenta frases com imperativo ("Take the subway", "Walk two blocks") e preposições ("on the corner", "across the bridge") -- gramática direta da aula!

Dica: ouça e pratique dar direções: "Go straight on this street. Turn right at the corner. The subway is two blocks away."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Greg -- "Asking for and Giving Directions in English" (14 min)

Greg ensina a pedir e dar direções usando o vocabulário da aula: "Excuse me, where is the subway?", "Turn left at the corner", "Walk across the bridge", "It's two blocks on the right". Pratica imperativo ("Turn", "Walk", "Go") + preposições ("on", "at", "across") com mapas visuais e exemplos reais.

Dica: use o mapa do seu bairro e pratique dar direções em inglês: "Walk down this street, turn right at the corner..."

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 5

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: Restaurante e pedidos de comida (menu, order, appetizer, main course, dessert, bill, tip, waiter)

Filme
Julie and Julia (2009) -- Cena do jantar no restaurante francês (minuto 42-55)

Julie pede o menu, escolhe appetizer e main course, e conversa com o waiter. Frases como "Could I see the menu?" e "I would like the..." aparecem naturalmente. Vocabulário direto da aula!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Preste atenção nas cenas de restaurante e anote como pedem a conta (bill) e deixam gorjeta (tip).

Podcast
All Ears English -- Episode 1547: "How to Order Food Like a Native Speaker"

Episódio 1547: frases reais para pedir comida como "Could I have the grilled fish, please?" e "I would like an appetizer to start." Praticam exatamente as estruturas da aula com menu, order e bill!

Dica: ouça o episódio e repita as frases de pedido em voz alta. Compare com as frases da sua aula.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy -- "30 MUST-KNOW Restaurant Phrases" (12 min)

Lucy ensina frases como "Could I see the menu, please?", "Is the tip included?" e "Could I have the bill?" Exatamente o vocabulário da aula: waiter, dessert, main course e appetizer!

Dica: pause o vídeo a cada frase e repita em voz alta. Grave-se dizendo "Could I have the bill, please?" e compare com a Lucy!

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 6

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: Compras e souvenirs (price, size, souvenir, cash, credit card, discount, receipt, change)

Filme
Confessions of a Shopaholic (2009) -- Cena da loja de lenços (minuto 8-15)

Rebecca pergunta "How much is this scarf?", compara preços ("This one is cheaper than that one"), pede discount e paga com credit card. Vocabulário idêntico ao da aula: price, size, receipt e change!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Observe como ela pergunta preços e negocia descontos, usando frases como "Is there a discount?".

Podcast
All Ears English -- Episode 1632: "How to Bargain and Get a Discount in English"

Episódio 1632: frases reais para pedir desconto como "Is there a discount if I buy two?" e perguntar preços com "How much is this?". Praticam comparativos (cheaper, more expensive) exatamente como na aula!

Dica: ouça o episódio e repita as frases sobre preços e pagamento. Compare com o diálogo da Tânia na loja de souvenirs.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy -- "Shopping in English: Essential Phrases for Tourists" (10 min)

Lucy ensina frases para compras em viagem: "Do you have this in a larger size?", "Could I pay with a credit card?" e "Could I have the receipt, please?". Vocabulário direto da aula: souvenir, cash, discount!

Dica: pause o vídeo a cada frase e repita. Pratique perguntar "How much is this?" e "Is there a discount?" em voz alta!

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 7

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Tema da aula: Cultura e tradições em viagens (tradition, festival, monument, local, custom, heritage, landmark, diversity + Present Perfect)

Série
An Idiot Abroad -- Season 1, Episode 2: "India" (Sky One)

Karl visita a Índia e descobre traditions e customs locais. Ele usa frases como "I have never been to..." e "The locals were very friendly." Vocabulário da aula: festival, heritage, diversity e landmark aparecem naturalmente!

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 5 traditions ou customs que Karl menciona e pratique descrever com Present Perfect: "He has visited...".

Podcast
English Learning for Curious Minds -- Episode 267: "The Story of Carnival in Brazil" (Leonardo English)

Episódio 267: conta a história do Carnaval brasileiro como tradition e heritage cultural. Usa frases como "Brazil has a rich cultural heritage" e "Every region has different customs", exatamente como no diálogo da aula!

Dica: ouça o episódio e pratique descrever tradições brasileiras usando Present Perfect: "I have been to Carnival" e "I have visited many festivals."

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
TED-Ed -- "What makes Rome so special?" (5 min) + "The world's most famous landmarks" (4 min)

Dois vídeos curtos sobre landmarks e monuments famosos como o Colosseum e a Eiffel Tower, exatamente como na aula! Vocabulário: monument, landmark, heritage e diversity. Frases como "The Eiffel Tower is a famous monument" aparecem nos vídeos.

Dica: depois de assistir, escreva 3 frases usando Present Perfect: "I have visited...", "I have never been to...", "I have learned about...".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 8

Conteúdos para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Série
The Good Doctor — Season 1

Série médica com vocabulário de saúde em contexto real. Ideal para ouvir termos como symptoms, medicine, doctor, emergency em situações autênticas.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 5 palavras médicas que ouvir.

Podcast
6 Minute English BBC — Health

Episódios curtos sobre tópicos de saúde. Pronúncia clara e acessível para A2. Ideal para praticar vocabulário médico em contexto.

Dica: ouça um episódio sobre saúde e pratique as frases do Survival Card.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
English with Lucy — At the Doctor

Vídeo prático com frases para consultas médicas e farmácias. Vocabulário acessível e exemplos reais de conversas de saúde.

Dica: assista e pratique descrevendo seus sintomas em inglês em voz alta.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 9

Tema da aula: Family Travel — fazer sugestões e coordenar uma viagem com a família. Preste atenção em como as pessoas usam "let's", "shall we", "why don't we" e "how about" para combinar planos. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Série
Modern Family — Season 3, Episode 22: "Disneyland"

A família inteira viaja junta para a Disneyland e precisa se organizar o tempo todo. Preste atenção nas frases para sugerir o que fazer: "Let's go on that ride", "Why don't we meet here?", "How about we split up?" — exatamente as estruturas da aula, com vocabulário de família (cousins, kids, grandparents).

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês. Anote 5 sugestões ("Let's...", "How about...") que ouvir e copie o padrão.

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) — Travel & Holidays

Episódios curtos sobre viagens e planos. Pronúncia clara e acessível para A2. Procure um episódio sobre "holidays" ou "travel" e ouça como falam de planejar a viagem e fazer sugestões.

Dica: ouça um episódio sobre viagem e pratique as 5 frases do Survival Card desta aula em voz alta.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Making Suggestions and Replies — let's, shall we, why don't we

Vídeo de inglês com exemplos diretos das estruturas da aula: "Let's...", "Shall we...?", "Why don't we...?" e como aceitar ou recusar uma sugestão. Perfeito para A2.

Dica: assista, pause em cada exemplo e repita em voz alta. Depois invente uma sugestão sua para uma viagem em família.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 10

Tema da aula: revisão geral — uma viagem inteira em inglês (aeroporto, hotel, direções, restaurante, compras, cultura, emergências, família). Preste atenção em como os personagens usam as estruturas do curso em situações reais. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Série
Emily in Paris — Season 1, Episode 1: "Emily in Paris"

Emily se muda para Paris e precisa se virar em tudo: aeroporto, apartamento, restaurante, trabalho e cidade nova. É a sua viagem inteira em forma de série — repare nas frases de check-in, pedir comida, pedir ajuda e se apresentar.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase de cada situação (aeroporto, hotel, restaurante) que combine com o que você aprendeu.

Podcast
6 Minute English (BBC) — Travel & Everyday English

Episódios curtos com conversas do dia a dia e de viagem. Pronúncia clara para A2. Ideal para revisar vocabulário e estruturas do curso ouvindo em contexto real.

Dica: ouça um episódio sobre viagem e repita em voz alta as 5 frases do Survival Card desta aula.

Listen on Spotify ↗
YouTube
Making Suggestions and Replies — let's, shall we, why don't we

Vídeo de revisão das sugestões (Aula 9) e de como aceitar ou recusar planos — útil pra fechar o curso treinando a conversa de planejar viagens.

Dica: assista, pause e invente uma sugestão pra cada lugar da viagem (hotel, restaurante, passeio).

Watch on YouTube ↗

Materiais Complementares — Aula 11

Tema da aula: small talk — conversar com desconhecidos durante as viagens. Foco: question forms e tag questions. Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 11 -- Small Talk

Série / Filme
"Before Sunrise" (1995) -- two strangers start talking on a train

Um clássico sobre dois desconhecidos que começam a conversar em um trem na Europa. Modelo perfeito de small talk natural entre viajantes. Conexão com a aula: quebrar o gelo e manter a conversa fluindo.

Dica: assista à cena de abertura com legenda em inglês. Anote 3 perguntas que eles usam para se conhecer.

Podcast
BBC Learning English -- "The English We Speak" (small talk & idioms)

Episódios curtos que explicam expressões do dia a dia usadas em conversas informais, incluindo icebreakers e como reagir naturalmente. Ideal para o nível A2 / B1.

Dica: ouça um episódio e treine repetir as tag questions com a mesma entonação.

YouTube
"Tag Questions in English -- isn't it? aren't you?"

Vídeo que explica como formar e pronunciar tag questions, com a entonação certa (subindo para perguntar, descendo para confirmar). Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 tag questions sobre a sua próxima viagem.

Materiais Complementares — Aula 12

Tema da aula: compartilhar opiniões — concordar e discordar com educação durante as viagens. Foco: linguagem de opinião e respostas curtas (so do I / neither do I). Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 12 -- Sharing Opinions

Série / Filme
"Chef" (2014) -- friends and family debate food and travel

Um filme leve sobre comida, viagens e pessoas com opiniões fortes. Cheio de conversas em que os personagens concordam e discordam de forma natural. Conexão com a aula: trocar opiniões e preferencias.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote 3 frases que os personagens usam para concordar ou discordar.

Podcast
BBC Learning English -- "6 Minute English" (giving opinions)

Episódios curtos em que dois apresentadores discutem um tema, dando suas opiniões e concordando ou discordando com educação. Ideal para ouvir a linguagem real de opinião em nível A2 / B1.

Dica: ouça um episódio e treine repetir "In my opinion" e "I'm not sure about that".

YouTube
"Agreeing and Disagreeing in English -- So do I / Neither do I"

Vídeo que explica como reagir a opiniões: "So do I", "Neither do I" e como discordar com educação. Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 reações ("So do I" / "Neither do I") sobre as suas próprias preferências de viagem.

Materiais Complementares — Aula 13

Tema da aula: fazer planos — falar sobre a próxima viagem com going to (planos) e will (decisões de momento). Foco: futuro e vocabulário de planejamento (book, budget, packing list). Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 13 -- Making Plans

Série / Filme
"The Bucket List" (2007) -- two friends plan the trips of their dreams

Dois amigos fazem uma lista de tudo o que vão fazer e viajam pelo mundo. Cheio de frases sobre planos futuros ("we are going to...", "I will..."). Conexão com a aula: planejar e falar sobre o futuro.

Dica: anote 3 planos que os personagens fazem e reescreva cada um com "going to".

Podcast
BBC Learning English -- "English at Work / Making Plans"

Episódios curtos em que as pessoas fazem planos e tomam decisões, usando "going to" e "will" o tempo todo. Ideal para ouvir a diferença entre plano e decisão de momento em nível A2 / B1.

Dica: ouça um episódio e repita em voz alta cada "I am going to" e cada "I will" que ouvir.

YouTube
"Going to vs Will -- Talking About the Future in English"

Vídeo que explica quando usar "going to" (planos) e quando usar "will" (decisões de momento e promessas). Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 planos para a sua próxima viagem usando "I am going to".

Materiais Complementares — Aula 14

Tema da aula: contar histórias de viagem — descrever o que estava acontecendo no passado com o past continuous (was/were + -ing) e a surpresa com when + past simple. Foco: past continuous e vocabulário de histórias (adventure, view, suddenly, journey). Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 14 -- Travel Stories

Série / Filme
"The Secret Life of Walter Mitty" (2013) -- one big travel adventure

Um homem comum vive uma aventura incrível viajando pelo mundo, cheia de paisagens lindas. Perfeito para ouvir histórias no passado e descrições de cenário. Conexão com a aula: contar a história de uma viagem.

Dica: escolha uma cena e descreva o que o personagem estava fazendo usando "he was + -ing".

Podcast
BBC Learning English -- "6 Minute English / Storytelling"

Episódios curtos em que as pessoas contam histórias e experiências do passado, usando o past continuous para montar o cenário. Ideal para ouvir "was/were + -ing" em contexto, nível A2 / B1.

Dica: anote 3 frases com "I was..." ou "they were..." que você ouvir.

YouTube
"Past Continuous vs Past Simple -- Telling Stories in English"

Vídeo que explica quando usar o past continuous (ação em progresso, cenário) e quando usar o past simple (ação curta que interrompe). Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 frases sobre a sua última viagem usando "I was + -ing".

Materiais Complementares — Aula 15

Tema da aula: descrever pessoas e lugares — pintar o quadro de uma viagem com adjetivos na ordem certa (opinion > size > age > color). Foco: ordem dos adjetivos e vocabulário de descrição (tall, narrow, crowded, stunning, cozy). Marque como concluído ao terminar.

Aula 15 -- Describing People & Places

Série / Filme
"Call Me by Your Name" (2017) -- a beautiful old Italian town

Um filme cheio de descrições de pessoas e de uma cidadezinha antiga e charmosa na Itália: ruas estreitas, praças tranquilas e paisagens deslumbrantes. Conexão com a aula: descrever pessoas e lugares.

Dica: escolha uma cena e descreva o lugar com três adjetivos na ordem certa (opinion > size > age).

Podcast
BBC Learning English -- "6 Minute English / Describing Places"

Episódios curtos em que as pessoas descrevem cidades, ruas e pessoas usando muitos adjetivos. Ideal para ouvir adjetivos em sequência (crowded, peaceful, ancient) em contexto, nível A2 / B1.

Dica: anote 3 adjetivos novos que você ouvir e diga uma frase com cada um.

YouTube
"Order of Adjectives in English -- Describing People & Places"

Vídeo que explica a ordem natural dos adjetivos (opinion > size > age > color) com muitos exemplos de pessoas e lugares. Revisão direta do foco da aula.

Dica: pause o vídeo e crie 5 frases descrevendo lugares da sua cidade com dois adjetivos.

The Dream Words The Code Getting There Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1

Who Is
Tânia Rosa?

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome, Tânia!

"Tell me: where do you live?"
"What countries have you visited?"
"Why do you want to improve your English?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

A World
Traveler

"Já se comunica... mas precisa melhorar vocabulário e pronúncias."

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently introduce herself in English, talk about her travels, and have a simple conversation with a stranger on a plane.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Travel
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for your next adventure

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To go on a journey to another place
Travel
To go on a journey to another place
"I love to travel to new places."
The place where you are going
Destination
The place where you are going
"My next destination is Portugal."
The document you need to enter a country
Passport
The document you need to enter a country
"Could I see your passport, please?"
Something that you have lived or done
Experience
Something that you have lived or done
"Living abroad is a great experience."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

The customs and way of life of a group
Culture
The customs and way of life of a group
"I enjoy learning about different cultures."
To go to a place to see it or spend time
Visit
To go to a place to see it or spend time
"I want to visit Japan next year."
A nation with its own government
Country
A nation with its own government and borders
"Brazil is a beautiful country."
The words people use to communicate
Language
The words and grammar people use to communicate
"English is an international language."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Travel"I love to travel to new places."
Destination"My next destination is Portugal."
Passport"Could I see your passport, please?"
Experience"Living abroad is a great experience."
Culture"I enjoy learning about different cultures."
Visit"I want to visit Japan next year."
Country"Brazil is a beautiful country."
Language"English is an international language."
Chapter 3: The Code

Present
Simple

Facts, habits, and routines

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

Tânia lives in Brazil.
She travels to many countries.
She does not stay home on vacation.
Does she enjoy different cultures?
FormStructureExample
AffirmativeSubject + verb (base/+s)She travels every year.
NegativeSubject + do/does not + verbShe does not speak French.
QuestionDo/Does + subject + verb?Does she like traveling?
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"She travel every year."
"She travels every year."
"Does she likes traveling?"
"Does she like traveling?"
Practice

Fill in the Verb

"Tânia ___lives in Brazil."
"She ___travels to many countries."
"She ___does not speak French."
"___Does she enjoy traveling?"
"I ___love visiting new destinations."
"They ___do not visit Europe often."
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia Meets
David

A conversation on the flight to Europe

Artifact

Your Travel Passport

TRAVEL PASSPORT
Tânia Rosa
World Traveler — Cosmopolitan
Brazil
Countries visited: Portugal, Spain, France
Loves: Culture, family, piano, crochet
Dialogue

On the Flight

D
Hello! Are you traveling to Europe too?
T
Yes, I am! I am going to visit a few countries.
D
That sounds exciting! Where are you from?
T
I am from Brazil. I love traveling to new destinations.
D
Brazil! Do you travel often?
T
Yes, I do. I usually travel with my children and grandchildren.
D
That is wonderful! What countries have you visited?
T
I have been to Portugal, Spain, and France. I love different cultures.
D
Do you speak any other languages?
T
A little. I want to improve my English. It is important for traveling.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. Where is Tânia going?
She is going to visit a few countries in Europe.
2. Who does Tânia usually travel with?
She usually travels with her children and grandchildren.
3. What countries has Tânia visited?
She has been to Portugal, Spain, and France.
Listening

Flight Conversation

Listen to Tânia meeting David on the flight.

1. Does Tânia travel alone?
No, she usually travels with her children and grandchildren.
2. What does Tânia love about traveling?
She loves different cultures.
Listening

Listen for Grammar

Listen again. Focus on the Present Simple structures.

1. How does David ask about Tânia's travels?
"Do you travel often?"
2. How does David ask about languages?
"Do you speak any other languages?"
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Someone asks: "What is your name?" What do you say?
"My name is Tânia Rosa. Nice to meet you."
Detective

Spot the Error

"She travel every year."
"She travels every year."
"Does she likes traveling?"
"Does she like traveling?"
"I does not speak English."
"I do not speak English."
"Tânia live in Brazil."
"Tânia lives in Brazil."
"Do he visit other countries?"
"Does he visit other countries?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. Someone asks: "Where do you live?"
"I live in Brazil."
2. Tell David about your hobbies.
"I play piano and I do crochet."
3. Someone asks: "Do you like European food?"
"Yes, I do. I love trying new food."
4. Ask David about his job.
"What do you do?"
5. Tell David about your travels.
"I have been to many countries. I love visiting new places."
6. Ask David about his family.
"Do you travel with your family?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

Three levels of practice — guided, semi-free, and free

Role-Play

Role-Play: Meet on the Flight

You are on a flight to Europe. The person next to you (David) starts a conversation. Introduce yourself and answer his questions.
I am fromI travelI visitculturefamilycountry
Role-Play

Role-Play: Hotel Lobby Chat

You meet another guest at the hotel lobby. Chat about yourself, your travel plans, and what you enjoy doing.
my name isI enjoydo you
Free Practice

Role-Play: Free Conversation

You are at a café in a new city. Have a complete conversation with a friendly local. Talk about yourself, your travels, your family, and your interests. Ask them questions too!

Use everything you learned today: travel, destination, passport, experience, culture, visit, country, language, Present Simple.

Feedback

Delayed Error Correction

Your teacher will share 3-5 corrections from the role-plays. Repeat each corrected version twice.

Teacher notes corrections here during the role-plays...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Hi, I am Tânia Rosa. I am from Brazil."
2
"I love traveling. I have been to many countries."
3
"I usually travel with my children and grandchildren."
4
"I enjoy visiting new destinations and learning about cultures."
5
"Could you repeat that, please? I am learning English."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself in English.
I know 8 travel vocabulary words.
I can use Present Simple for facts and habits.
I can ask questions with "Do you...?"
I can answer questions about myself and my travels.
I can talk about my family and hobbies.
I can have a simple conversation with a stranger.
I feel more confident about speaking English when I travel.
Lesson Complete

Day 1 — Complete!

Next lesson: At the Airport — Navigating Your Way Through International Terminals

Lesson 2

At the
Airport

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome back, Tânia!

"Do you remember: what is a passport?"
"Last class you said your destination is... where?"
"Have you ever been to an international airport alone?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Flying to
Paris

Navigating international terminals with confidence

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently navigate an international airport — check in, find her gate, and handle any situation in English using polite requests.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Airport
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for navigating any airport

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A journey by airplane
Flight
A journey by airplane
"My flight to London departs at 10 AM."
The document you need to board the plane
Boarding Pass
The document you need to board the plane
"Could I see your boarding pass, please?"
The area where you wait to board
Gate
The area where you wait to board
"Your gate is B12."
Bags and suitcases for a trip
Luggage
Bags and suitcases for a trip
"I need to check my luggage."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

Registering at the airport counter
Check-in
Registering at the airport counter
"Could I check in for my flight, please?"
The main building at an airport
Terminal
The main building at an airport
"The international terminal is on the left."
When the plane leaves
Departure
When the plane leaves
"The departure time is 3:30 PM."
When the plane reaches the destination
Arrival
When the plane reaches the destination
"The arrival time in Paris is 8 PM."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Flight"My flight to London departs at 10 AM."
Boarding Pass"Could I see your boarding pass, please?"
Gate"Your gate is B12."
Luggage"I need to check my luggage."
Check-in"Could I check in for my flight, please?"
Terminal"The international terminal is on the left."
Departure"The departure time is 3:30 PM."
Arrival"The arrival time in Paris is 8 PM."
Callback

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 1 — you will need these at the airport too!

TravelTo go on a journey
DestinationThe place where you are going
PassportThe document you need to enter a country
CountryA nation with its own government
Chapter 3: The Code

Can / Could

Polite requests at the airport

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

Could I have a window seat, please?
Can you help me with my luggage?
I could not find my gate.
Could you tell me where Terminal B is?
FormStructureExample
RequestCould I + verb...?Could I have a window seat?
Asking helpCan/Could you + verb...?Can you help me?
NegativeSubject + could not + verbI could not find my gate.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I want a window seat."
"Could I have a window seat, please?"
"Where is gate?"
"Where is the gate?"
Practice

Fill in the Request

"___Could I have a window seat, please?"
"___Can you help me with my luggage?"
"I ___could not find my gate."
"___Could you tell me the departure time?"
"___Could I check in for my flight, please?"
"___Could you tell me where Terminal B is?"
Build

Build Your Sentences

Use "Could I..." or "Could you..." to make these polite:

Ask for an aisle seat: "___Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
Ask where gate B7 is: "___Could you tell me where gate B7 is?"
Ask for help with luggage: "___Could you help me with my luggage, please?"
Ask about departure time: "___Could you tell me the departure time, please?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia at the
Check-in Counter

A conversation at the airport

Artifact

Your Boarding Pass

International AirlinesFlight IA 2045
GRU
São Paulo
➔
CDG
Paris
PassengerROSA / TÂNIA
GateB7
Seat14A
ClassWindow
Departure3:30 PM
Board By3:00 PM
Dialogue

At the Check-in Counter

A
Good morning! Welcome to International Airlines. Could I see your passport, please?
T
Good morning! Here is my passport. I am flying to Paris.
A
Thank you, Mrs. Rosa. Could I see your booking confirmation?
T
Yes, here it is. Could I have a window seat, please?
A
Of course! I have seat 14A for you. Are you checking any luggage today?
T
Yes, I have one suitcase. How much luggage can I take?
A
You can check one bag up to 23 kilos. Could I see your luggage?
T
Here it is. Could you tell me where my gate is?
A
Your gate is B7. Departure is at 3:30 PM. Here is your boarding pass.
T
Thank you very much! Could you tell me where Terminal B is?
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What seat does Tânia get?
She gets seat 14A, a window seat.
2. What is the departure time?
The departure time is 3:30 PM.
3. What gate does Tânia need to go to?
She needs to go to gate B7.
Listening

Check-in Conversation

Listen to Tânia checking in at the airport.

1. Does Tânia check any luggage?
Yes, she has one suitcase.
2. How does Tânia ask for a window seat?
"Could I have a window seat, please?"
Listening

Listen for Polite Requests

Listen again. Count how many times you hear "Could I" or "Could you".

1. How does the agent ask for the passport?
"Could I see your passport, please?"
2. How does Tânia ask about the gate?
"Could you tell me where my gate is?"
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Click on each card to reveal the answer.

1. At check-in, they ask for your passport. What do you say?
"Here is my passport."
2. You want a window seat. How do you ask politely?
"Could I have a window seat, please?"
3. You cannot find your gate. How do you ask for help?
"Excuse me, could you tell me where gate B7 is?"
4. You want to know the departure time. How do you ask?
"Could you tell me the departure time, please?"
5. You need help with your luggage. What do you say?
"Could you help me with my luggage, please?"
6. Someone asks: "Can I help you?" What do you say?
"Yes, please. I am looking for Terminal B."
Detective

Spot the Error

"I want a window seat."
"Could I have a window seat, please?"
"Where is gate?"
"Where is the gate?"
"Can I sees your passport?"
"Can I see your passport?"
"She could not finds her luggage."
"She could not find her luggage."
"The flight depart at 3 PM."
"The flight departs at 3 PM."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. You arrive at check-in. Greet the agent.
"Good morning! I am checking in for my flight to Paris."
2. Ask politely for an aisle seat.
"Could I have an aisle seat, please?"
3. Ask where Terminal B is.
"Excuse me, could you tell me where Terminal B is?"
4. Someone asks about your destination.
"I am flying to Paris. My flight departs at 3:30 PM."
5. Ask about luggage weight limit.
"How much luggage can I take, please?"
6. Thank the agent and ask about the gate.
"Thank you! Could you tell me where gate B7 is?"
Build

Fix and Improve

Make these sentences polite and correct:

"I want see your passport." ➔ "___Could I see your passport, please?"
"Tell me where gate is." ➔ "___Could you tell me where the gate is?"
"I want window seat." ➔ "___Could I have a window seat, please?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

Three levels of practice — guided, semi-free, and free

Role-Play

Role-Play: Airport Check-in

You are at the airport check-in counter. Check in for your flight to Paris.
Could Ipassportboarding passwindow seatluggagegate
Role-Play

Role-Play: Lost in the Airport

You are lost in the airport terminal. Ask people for directions to your gate.
excuse mecould youterminalgate
Free Practice

Role-Play: Flight Delayed

Your flight is delayed. Talk to the airline agent and other passengers about the situation. Ask about the new departure time, request information, and practice being polite under pressure.

Use everything you learned today: Could I, Could you, flight, gate, boarding pass, luggage, terminal, departure, arrival.

Feedback

Delayed Error Correction

Your teacher will share 3-5 corrections from the role-plays. Repeat each corrected version twice.

Teacher notes corrections here during the role-plays...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Your Boarding Pass Review

Read your boarding pass out loud:

International AirlinesFlight IA 2045
GRU
São Paulo
➔
CDG
Paris
PassengerROSA / TÂNIA
GateB7
Seat14A
ClassWindow
Departure3:30 PM
Board By3:00 PM
Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Could I check in for my flight, please?"
2
"Could I have a window seat, please?"
3
"Where is gate B7?"
4
"Could you help me with my luggage, please?"
5
"Could you tell me the departure time?"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can check in at an airport counter in English.
I know 8 airport vocabulary words.
I can make polite requests with "Could I...?"
I can ask for directions in the airport.
I can read a boarding pass in English.
I can ask about luggage and seats politely.
I can have a conversation at the check-in counter.
I feel more confident about navigating airports in English.

Airport Navigator

Badge earned! You can navigate any airport in the world.

Lesson Complete

Day 2 — Complete!

Next lesson: Hotel Check-In — Booking and Settling In

Coming Up Next

Lesson 3: Hotel Check-In

Booking and Settling In — "Would like" for polite preferences, reservation vocabulary, and hotel conversation.

Thank You

See you
next class!

Alumni by Better — Travel English

Lesson 3

Hotel
Check-In

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome back, Tânia!

"Last class you navigated the airport. What is a boarding pass?"
"Do you remember: what is a gate?"
"Today you arrive at your hotel. What do you say first?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Arriving at
Your Hotel

Booking and settling into your home abroad

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently check into a hotel, ask about her room, and handle any accommodation situation in English using polite requests.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Hotel
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for any hotel stay

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A booking for a room
Reservation
A booking for a room
"I have a reservation under the name Rosa."
The space where you sleep at the hotel
Room
The space where you sleep at the hotel
"Could I have a room with a view, please?"
The card or object to open your room
Key
The card or object to open your room
"Here is your room key. You are in room 405."
The time you must leave the hotel
Checkout
The time you must leave the hotel
"Checkout is at 11 AM."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

The person at the hotel front desk
Receptionist
The person at the hotel front desk
"The receptionist was very helpful."
The machine that takes you up and down floors
Elevator
The machine that takes you up and down floors
"The elevator is on your right."
The first meal of the day
Breakfast
The first meal of the day
"Would you like breakfast included?"
A level in a building
Floor
A level in a building
"Your room is on the fourth floor."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Reservation"I have a reservation under the name Rosa."
Room"Could I have a room with a view, please?"
Key"Here is your room key. You are in room 405."
Checkout"Checkout is at 11 AM."
Receptionist"The receptionist was very helpful."
Elevator"The elevator is on your right."
Breakfast"Would you like breakfast included?"
Floor"Your room is on the fourth floor."
Callback

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 2 — your airport journey brought you here!

FlightA journey by airplane
Boarding PassThe document you need to board the plane
GateThe area where you wait to board
Check-inRegistering at a counter
Chapter 3: The Code

Would Like

Polite requests and preferences at the hotel

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

I would like a room with a view, please.
Would you like breakfast included?
I would not like a room near the elevator.
Yes, I would. / No, thank you.
FormStructureExample
RequestI would like + nounI would like a room with a view.
QuestionWould you like + noun?Would you like breakfast?
NegativeI would not like + nounI would not like a noisy room.
Short answerYes, I would. / No, thank you.Yes, I would.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I want a room with view."
"I would like a room with a view, please."
"My room is in fourth floor."
"My room is on the fourth floor."
Practice

Fill in the Preference

"___I would like a room with a view, please."
"___Would you like breakfast included?"
"I ___would not like a room near the elevator."
"Would you like a double room?" — "Yes, ___I would."
"___I would like to know the checkout time, please."
"___Would you like a room on a high floor?"
Build

Build Your Sentences

Use "I would like..." or "Would you like...?" to make these polite:

You want a room on a high floor: "___I would like a room on a high floor, please."
Ask about Wi-Fi: "___Could you tell me the Wi-Fi password, please?"
Ask for a restaurant recommendation: "___Could you recommend a good restaurant nearby?"
Ask about breakfast time: "___What time is breakfast served?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia at the
Hotel Front Desk

A conversation at the Grand Palace Hotel

Artifact

Your Hotel Confirmation

Grand Palace HotelConf. #GPH-2026-0520
GuestTÂNIA ROSA
Room405
Room TypeDouble, City View
Floor4th
Check-inMay 20, 2026
CheckoutMay 23, 2026 (11:00 AM)
BreakfastIncluded (7:00-10:00 AM)
Wi-FiGrandPalace_Guest
Dialogue

At the Front Desk

R
Good evening! Welcome to the Grand Palace Hotel. How can I help you?
T
Good evening! I have a reservation under the name Tânia Rosa.
R
Let me check. Yes, Mrs. Rosa! A double room for three nights. Is that correct?
T
Yes, that is correct. I would like a room with a view, if possible.
R
Of course! Room 405 on the fourth floor has a lovely city view. Would you like breakfast included?
T
Yes, I would. What time is breakfast served?
R
Breakfast is from 7 to 10 AM in the restaurant on the first floor.
T
That sounds perfect. What time is checkout?
R
Checkout is at 11 AM. Here is your room key. The elevator is on your right.
T
Thank you very much! Could you also recommend a good restaurant nearby?
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What kind of room does Tânia want?
She would like a room with a view.
2. What time is breakfast?
Breakfast is from 7 to 10 AM.
3. Where is the elevator?
The elevator is on the right.
Listening

Hotel Check-in Conversation

Listen to Tânia checking in at the hotel.

1. Does Tânia want breakfast included?
Yes, she would.
2. How does Tânia ask for a room with a view?
"I would like a room with a view, if possible."
Listening

Listen for Would Like

Listen again. Count how many times you hear "would like".

1. How does Tânia express her preference for a room?
"I would like a room with a view, if possible."
2. How does the receptionist offer breakfast?
"Would you like breakfast included?"
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Click on each card to reveal the answer.

1. You arrive at the hotel. What do you say?
"Good evening! I have a reservation under the name Tânia Rosa."
2. You want a room with a view. How do you ask politely?
"I would like a room with a view, please."
3. The receptionist asks if you want breakfast. What do you say?
"Yes, I would. Thank you."
4. You want to know the checkout time. What do you ask?
"What time is checkout, please?"
5. You cannot find the elevator. How do you ask?
"Excuse me, where is the elevator?"
6. You want a restaurant recommendation. What do you say?
"Could you recommend a good restaurant nearby?"
Detective

Spot the Error

"I want a room with view."
"I would like a room with a view, please."
"What time is the checkout?"
"What time is checkout?"
"The receptionist give me the key."
"The receptionist gave me the key."
"Would you likes breakfast?"
"Would you like breakfast?"
"My room is in fourth floor."
"My room is on the fourth floor."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. You arrive at the hotel front desk. Greet the receptionist.
"Good evening! I have a reservation under the name Rosa."
2. Ask for a room on a high floor.
"I would like a room on a high floor, please."
3. Ask about breakfast time.
"What time is breakfast served?"
4. Ask where the elevator is.
"Excuse me, where is the elevator?"
5. Ask for the Wi-Fi password.
"Could you tell me the Wi-Fi password, please?"
6. Thank the receptionist and ask about checkout.
"Thank you! What time is checkout?"
Build

Fix and Improve

Make these sentences polite and correct:

"I want room with view." ➔ "___I would like a room with a view, please."
"Tell me checkout time." ➔ "___What time is checkout, please?"
"I want breakfast." ➔ "___I would like breakfast included, please."
Compare

Lesson 2 vs Lesson 3

Lesson 2: Could I

Asking permission / Making requests

"Could I have a window seat?"

Lesson 3: Would Like

Expressing preferences / Desires politely

"I would like a room with a view."

Mixed Practice

Choose: Could I or Would Like?

You want to see the menu: "___Could I see the menu, please?"
You prefer a quiet room: "___I would like a quiet room, please."
You want to check in: "___Could I check in, please?"
You prefer breakfast in the room: "___I would like breakfast in my room, please."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

Three levels of practice — guided, semi-free, and free

Role-Play

Role-Play: Hotel Check-in in Paris

You arrive at a hotel in Paris. Check in and ask about your room.
reservationwould likeroomviewbreakfastcheckoutfloor
Role-Play

Role-Play: Room Problem

You have a problem with your room (no hot water or too noisy). Talk to the receptionist.
I would likecould youroom
Free Practice

Role-Play: Breakfast Chat

You meet another guest at the hotel breakfast. Chat about your travels, your room, and your plans for the day.

Use everything you learned today: would like, reservation, room, breakfast, floor, checkout, elevator, key.

Feedback

Delayed Error Correction

Your teacher will share 3-5 corrections from the role-plays. Repeat each corrected version twice.

Teacher notes corrections here during the role-plays...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"I have a reservation under the name Rosa."
2
"I would like a room with a view, please."
3
"What time is breakfast served?"
4
"What time is checkout?"
5
"Could you recommend a good restaurant nearby?"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can check into a hotel in English.
I know 8 hotel vocabulary words.
I can make polite requests with "I would like..."
I can ask about hotel services (breakfast, checkout, elevator).
I can read a hotel confirmation in English.
I can ask for recommendations politely.
I can have a conversation at a hotel front desk.
I feel more confident about staying at hotels abroad.

Hotel Guest

Badge earned! You can check in at any hotel in the world.

Lesson Complete

Day 3 — Complete!

Next lesson: Getting Around — Directions and Transportation

Thank You

See you
next class!

Alumni by Better — Travel English

Lesson 4

Getting Around
the City

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome back, Tânia!

"Last class you checked into your hotel. What is a reservation?"
"Do you remember: what does the receptionist do?"
"Today you explore the city. How do you ask for directions?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Exploring
the City

Asking for directions and using transportation

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently ask for directions, understand responses, and navigate any city using public transportation in English.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

City
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for navigating any city

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

The way to go somewhere
Direction
The way to go somewhere
"Could you tell me the direction to the museum?"
A road in a city with buildings
Street
A road in a city with buildings
"The restaurant is on Main Street."
Where two streets meet
Corner
Where two streets meet
"Turn right at the corner."
The distance between two streets
Block
The distance between two streets
"The pharmacy is two blocks from here."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

Underground train system
Subway
Underground train system
"Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
A drawing that shows streets and places
Map
A drawing that shows streets and places
"I need a map of the city."
A structure that crosses over water
Bridge
A structure that crosses over water
"Walk across the bridge and turn left."
The path next to the street for walking
Sidewalk
The path next to the street for walking
"Stay on the sidewalk and go straight."
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Direction"Could you tell me the direction to the museum?"
Street"The restaurant is on Main Street."
Corner"Turn right at the corner."
Block"The pharmacy is two blocks from here."
Subway"Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
Map"I need a map of the city."
Bridge"Walk across the bridge and turn left."
Sidewalk"Stay on the sidewalk and go straight."
Callback

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 3 — your hotel is your base for exploring!

ReservationA booking for a room
RoomThe space where you sleep at the hotel
CheckoutThe time you must leave the hotel
ReceptionistThe person at the hotel front desk
Chapter 3: The Code

Imperative +
Prepositions

Giving and understanding directions

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

Go straight for two blocks.
Turn left at the corner.
The museum is next to the café.
The bank is across from the bridge.
TypeStructureExample
ImperativeGo / Turn / Walk / TakeGo straight for two blocks.
next tobesideThe museum is next to the café.
across fromon the other sideThe bank is across from the bridge.
on the corner ofat the intersectionThe pharmacy is on the corner of Main Street.
in front offacingThe bus stop is in front of the hotel.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"Go to the left at the corner."
"Turn left at the corner."
"The bank is in the corner."
"The bank is on the corner."
Practice

Fill in the Direction

"___Go straight for two blocks."
"___Turn left at the corner."
"The museum is ___next to the café."
"The bank is ___across from the bridge."
"___Walk across the bridge and turn left."
"The pharmacy is ___on the corner of Main Street."
Build

Build Your Sentences

Use imperatives and prepositions to give directions:

Tell someone to go straight 3 blocks: "___Go straight for three blocks."
The museum is beside the café: "___The museum is next to the café."
Ask where the subway is: "___Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
Ask for a map: "___Could I have a map of the city, please?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia Explores
the City

A conversation about finding the Art Museum

Artifact

Your City Map

Route: Hotel to Art Museum

Hotel
Bridge
Art Museum
Café
Main Street
→ 2 blocks →
↓ turn left
→ bridge →
Dialogue

Asking for Directions

T
Excuse me, could you help me? I am looking for the Art Museum.
L
Of course! The Art Museum is not far from here.
T
How do I get there?
L
Go straight on this street for two blocks.
T
Two blocks. And then?
L
Then turn left at the corner. You will see a big bridge.
T
Turn left at the corner, near the bridge. Got it.
L
The museum is across from the bridge, next to a café.
T
Across from the bridge, next to a café. Is it far to walk?
L
No, about ten minutes. You can also take the subway. The station is on the next block.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. Where is the Art Museum?
The museum is across from the bridge, next to a café.
2. How far is it to walk?
About ten minutes.
3. What is another way to get there?
You can take the subway. The station is on the next block.
Listening

Directions Conversation

Listen to Tânia asking for directions.

1. How many blocks does Tânia walk straight?
Two blocks.
2. Which direction does she turn at the corner?
She turns left.
Listening

Listen for Prepositions

Listen again. Count how many prepositions of place you hear.

1. What preposition tells you the museum's position relative to the bridge?
"Across from" — the museum is across from the bridge.
2. What is next to the museum?
A café — the museum is next to a café.
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Click on each card to reveal the answer.

1. You are lost. Ask for help.
"Excuse me, could you help me? I am looking for the Art Museum."
2. Someone says "Turn left." What do you do?
"I turn to the left side."
3. Find the subway.
"Excuse me, could you tell me where the subway station is?"
4. Confirm the hotel is 2 blocks away.
"Is the hotel two blocks from here?"
5. Ask for a map.
"Could I have a map of the city, please?"
6. Where is the bank? It is next to the pharmacy.
"The bank is next to the pharmacy, on Main Street."
Detective

Spot the Error

"Go to the left at the corner."
"Turn left at the corner."
"The museum is in the corner."
"The museum is on the corner."
"Walk straight for two block."
"Walk straight for two blocks."
"Where is the subway is?"
"Where is the subway?"
"The café is across the bridge."
"The café is across from the bridge."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. You need to find the subway station. Ask a stranger.
"Excuse me, could you tell me where the subway station is?"
2. Give directions: go straight 2 blocks, then turn right.
"Go straight for two blocks, then turn right."
3. Ask how far the museum is.
"How far is the museum from here?"
4. Say the bank is beside the pharmacy.
"The bank is next to the pharmacy."
5. Ask if you can walk there.
"Is it far to walk? Can I walk there?"
6. Ask for a map of the city.
"Could I have a map of the city, please?"
Build

Fix and Improve

Make these sentences correct:

"Go to the left at the corner." ➔ "___Turn left at the corner."
"The bank is in the corner." ➔ "___The bank is on the corner."
"The café is across the bridge." ➔ "___The café is across from the bridge."
Compare

Lesson 3 vs Lesson 4

Lesson 3: Would Like

Expressing preferences politely at the hotel

"I would like a room with a view."

Lesson 4: Imperative

Giving and understanding directions in the city

"Go straight, turn left at the corner."

Mixed Practice

Hotel or City?

You want a quiet room: "___I would like a quiet room, please."
Tell someone to go straight: "___Go straight for two blocks."
You want breakfast included: "___I would like breakfast included, please."
Ask where the subway is: "___Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

Three levels of practice — guided, semi-free, and free

Role-Play

Role-Play: Directions to the Museum

You are on the street. Ask a local person for directions to the Art Museum.
excuse mecould youturn leftgo straightblockscornernext to
Role-Play

Role-Play: Finding the Subway

You need to find the subway station. Ask someone on the street. Also ask which line to take.
subway stationwhich linehow far
Free Practice

Role-Play: Lost in the City

You are lost in a new city. You need to find three places: a museum, a restaurant, and the subway station. Ask different people for directions.

Use everything you learned today: direction, street, corner, block, subway, map, bridge, sidewalk, go straight, turn left/right, next to, across from.

Feedback

Delayed Error Correction

Your teacher will share 3-5 corrections from the role-plays. Repeat each corrected version twice.

Teacher notes corrections here during the role-plays...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Excuse me, could you help me?"
2
"How do I get to the Art Museum?"
3
"Turn left at the corner and go straight."
4
"Is it far to walk?"
5
"Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can ask for directions in English.
I know 8 city and direction vocabulary words.
I can use imperatives (Go, Turn, Walk, Take).
I can use prepositions of place (next to, across from, on the corner).
I can understand directions given by another person.
I can ask about public transportation.
I can navigate a city using English.
I feel more confident about exploring cities abroad.

City Navigator

Badge earned! You can navigate any city in the world.

Lesson Complete

Day 4 — Complete!

Next lesson: At the Restaurant — Ordering and the Bill

Thank You

See you
next class!

Alumni by Better — Travel English

Lesson 5

At the
Restaurant

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome back, Tânia!

"Last class you explored the city. What is a direction?"
"Do you remember: what is a subway?"
"Today you are hungry. How do you order food at a restaurant?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Dining
Out

Ordering food and handling the bill

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently order food, ask about the menu, and handle the bill at any restaurant in English.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Restaurant
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for dining out

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A list of food and drinks
Menu
A list of food and drinks at a restaurant
"Could I see the menu, please?"
To ask for food or drinks
Order
To ask for food or drinks
"Are you ready to order?"
A small dish before the main course
Appetizer
A small dish before the main course
"I would like an appetizer to start."
The biggest dish of the meal
Main Course
The biggest dish of the meal
"For the main course, I would like the grilled fish."
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A sweet dish after the meal
Dessert
A sweet dish after the meal
"Could I see the dessert menu, please?"
The paper that shows how much you must pay
Bill
The paper that shows how much you must pay
"Could I have the bill, please?"
Extra money you leave for the waiter
Tip
Extra money you leave for the waiter
"Is the tip included in the bill?"
The person who serves food at a restaurant
Waiter
The person who serves food at a restaurant
"Excuse me, could I ask the waiter a question?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Menu"Could I see the menu, please?"
Order"Are you ready to order?"
Appetizer"I would like an appetizer to start."
Main Course"For the main course, I would like the grilled fish."
Dessert"Could I see the dessert menu, please?"
Bill"Could I have the bill, please?"
Tip"Is the tip included in the bill?"
Waiter"Excuse me, could I ask the waiter a question?"
Callback

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 4 — you explored the city and found a restaurant!

DirectionThe way to go somewhere
StreetA road in a city with buildings
CornerWhere two streets meet
SubwayUnderground train system
Chapter 3: The Code

Could I Have +
Polite Ordering

The secret to ordering food with confidence

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

Could I have the grilled fish, please?
Could I see the menu?
Could we have a table for two?
Could you bring the bill, please?
StructureUseExample
Could I + verbPolite request for yourselfCould I have the fish?
Could we + verbPolite request for a groupCould we have a table for two?
Could you + verbAsking someone to do somethingCould you bring the bill?
I would like + nounStating a preference politelyI would like the grilled fish.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I want the fish."
"I would like the fish, please."
"Give me the bill."
"Could I have the bill, please?"
Practice

Complete the Order

"___Could I have the grilled fish, please?"
"___I would like a glass of sparkling water."
"___Could I see the dessert menu, please?"
"___Could you bring the bill, please?"
"___I would like an appetizer to start."
"___Could we have a table for two?"
Build

Build Your Orders

Use "Could I have..." or "I would like..." to order:

Ask to see the menu: "___Could I see the menu, please?"
Order the tomato soup: "___Could I have the tomato soup, please?"
Ask for the bill: "___Could I have the bill, please?"
Ask if tip is included: "___Is the tip included in the bill?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia at
La Bella Vista

A complete restaurant experience

Artifact

The Menu

La Bella Vista

Fine Italian Dining

Appetizers
Tomato Soup$8
Bruschetta$10
Caesar Salad$9
Main Courses
Grilled Fish$22
Pasta Primavera$18
Chicken Parmesan$20
Desserts
Tiramisu$10
Chocolate Cake$9
Fruit Salad$7
Drinks
Sparkling Water$3
Orange Juice$5
Coffee$4
Dialogue

Ordering at La Bella Vista

W
Good evening! Welcome to La Bella Vista. A table for one?
T
Good evening! Yes, a table for one, please. Could I sit near the window?
W
Of course! Here is the menu. Would you like something to drink?
T
I would like a glass of sparkling water, please.
W
Are you ready to order, or would you like a few more minutes?
T
I am ready. Could I have the tomato soup as an appetizer?
W
Excellent choice! And for the main course?
T
I would like the grilled fish with vegetables, please.
W
Perfect. Would you like to see the dessert menu later?
T
Yes, please. And could I have the bill after dessert? I am in no hurry.
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What does Tânia order as an appetizer?
She orders the tomato soup.
2. What is her main course?
The grilled fish with vegetables.
3. When does Tânia want the bill?
After dessert. She is in no hurry.
Listening

Restaurant Conversation

Listen to Tânia ordering at the restaurant.

1. What does Tânia order as an appetizer?
The tomato soup.
2. What does she order as a main course?
The grilled fish with vegetables.
Listening

Listen for Polite Expressions

Listen again. Count how many times Tânia uses "Could I have" or "I would like."

1. How many polite expressions does Tânia use?
Five: "Could I sit," "I would like water," "Could I have the soup," "I would like the fish," "Could I have the bill."
2. Does Tânia ever say "I want"?
No! She always uses "Could I have" or "I would like" — very polite!
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Click on each card to reveal the answer.

1. You arrive at a restaurant. Ask for a table.
"Good evening! Could I have a table for one, please?"
2. You want to see the menu.
"Could I see the menu, please?"
3. The waiter asks if you are ready. You want fish.
"Yes, I would like the grilled fish, please."
4. You want to know about desserts.
"Could I see the dessert menu, please?"
5. You are finished. Ask for the bill.
"Could I have the bill, please?"
6. You want to know if tip is included.
"Excuse me, is the tip included?"
Detective

Spot the Error

"I want the fish."
"I would like the fish, please."
"Give me the bill."
"Could I have the bill, please?"
"I would like a appetizer."
"I would like an appetizer."
"Could I has the menu?"
"Could I have the menu?"
"The waiter bring the dessert."
"The waiter brings the dessert."
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. Ask for a table for two.
"Good evening! Could I have a table for two, please?"
2. Order sparkling water.
"I would like a glass of sparkling water, please."
3. Ask about today's special.
"Excuse me, what is today's special?"
4. Order the soup and the fish.
"Could I have the tomato soup as an appetizer and the grilled fish as the main course?"
5. Ask for the bill.
"Could I have the bill, please?"
6. Ask if tip is included.
"Is the tip included in the bill?"
Build

Fix and Improve

Make these sentences polite and correct:

"I want the fish." ➔ "___I would like the fish, please."
"Give me the bill." ➔ "___Could I have the bill, please?"
"I want a appetizer." ➔ "___I would like an appetizer, please."
Compare

Lesson 4 vs Lesson 5

Lesson 4: Imperatives

Giving and understanding directions in the city

"Go straight, turn left at the corner."

Lesson 5: Could I Have

Ordering food politely at a restaurant

"Could I have the grilled fish, please?"

Mixed Practice

City or Restaurant?

You want the grilled fish: "___Could I have the grilled fish, please?"
Tell someone to go straight: "___Go straight for two blocks."
You want to see the menu: "___Could I see the menu, please?"
Ask where the subway is: "___Could you tell me where the subway station is?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak

Three levels of practice — guided, semi-free, and free

Role-Play

Role-Play: Order a Complete Meal

You are at a restaurant. Order a complete meal: appetizer, main course, dessert, and a drink.
could I haveI would likemenuappetizermain coursedessertbill
Role-Play

Role-Play: Wrong Dish

The waiter brought the wrong dish. You ordered fish but got chicken. Politely explain and ask for the correct one.
excuse meI orderedcould you
Free Practice

Role-Play: Dinner with a Friend

You are at a restaurant with a new friend you met at the hotel. Order food, chat, and pay the bill.

Use everything you learned today: menu, order, appetizer, main course, dessert, bill, tip, waiter, Could I have, I would like.

Feedback

Delayed Error Correction

Your teacher will share 3-5 corrections from the role-plays. Repeat each corrected version twice.

Teacher notes corrections here during the role-plays...

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

5 Key Phrases

1
"Could I see the menu, please?"
2
"I would like the grilled fish, please."
3
"Could I have the bill, please?"
4
"Is the tip included?"
5
"Thank you, the food was delicious!"
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can order food at a restaurant in English.
I know 8 restaurant vocabulary words.
I can use "Could I have..." for polite ordering.
I can read and understand a menu in English.
I can ask about dishes and menu options.
I can handle the bill and ask about the tip.
I can have a complete restaurant conversation.
I feel confident about dining abroad.

Restaurant Pro

Badge earned! You can dine at any restaurant in the world.

Lesson Complete

Day 5 — Complete!

Next lesson: Shopping and Souvenirs — Prices and Bargaining

Thank You

See you
next class!

Alumni by Better — Travel English

Lesson 6

Shopping and
Souvenirs

A2 — 60 minutes — Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Welcome back, Tânia!

"Last class you had a wonderful dinner. What is a menu?"
"Do you remember: what is a tip?"
"Today you go shopping! How do you ask about prices?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Going
Shopping

Prices, sizes, and bargaining

Today's Goal

After this lesson, Tânia will...

...confidently shop abroad — ask about prices, sizes, compare items, and pay in English.

Chapter 2: Packing Words

Shopping
Vocabulary

Eight essential words for shopping abroad

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

How much something costs
Price
How much something costs
"What is the price of this scarf?"
How big or small something is
Size
How big or small something is
"Do you have this in a larger size?"
Something you buy to remember a trip
Souvenir
Something you buy to remember a trip
"I would like to buy some souvenirs for my grandchildren."
Paper money and coins
Cash
Paper money and coins
"Could I pay in cash?"
0 / 4 words revealed
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

A plastic card for payment
Credit Card
A plastic card for payment
"Do you accept credit cards?"
A reduction in price
Discount
A reduction in price
"Is there a discount if I buy two?"
Proof of payment
Receipt
Proof of payment
"Could I have the receipt, please?"
Money returned after payment
Change
Money returned after payment
"Here is your change. Thank you!"
0 / 4 words revealed
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Price"What is the price of this scarf?"
Size"Do you have this in a larger size?"
Souvenir"I would like to buy some souvenirs for my grandchildren."
Cash"Could I pay in cash?"
Credit Card"Do you accept credit cards?"
Discount"Is there a discount if I buy two?"
Receipt"Could I have the receipt, please?"
Change"Here is your change. Thank you!"
Callback

Words You Already Know

From Lesson 5 — you had a wonderful dinner and now you go shopping!

MenuA list of food and drinks
OrderTo ask for food or drinks
BillThe paper that shows how much you must pay
TipExtra money you leave for the waiter
Chapter 3: The Code

How Much +
Comparatives

The secret to shopping with confidence

Grammar Discovery

Grammar Discovery

Look at the colored words. What do they have in common?

How much is this scarf?
How much does the bag cost?
This one is cheaper than that one.
The blue scarf is more expensive than the red one.
StructureFormExample
Asking price (be)How much + isHow much is this scarf?
Asking price (does)How much + does + costHow much does the bag cost?
Short adjectiveadj + -er + thanCheaper than / bigger than
Long adjectivemore + adj + thanMore expensive than
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"How much it costs?"
"How much does it cost?"
"This is more cheap."
"This is cheaper."
Practice

Complete the Questions

"___How much is this bag?"
"This one is ___cheaper than that one."
"The blue scarf is ___more expensive than the red one."
"Do you have this in a ___bigger size?"
"___How much does the jewelry cost?"
Build

Build Your Shopping Phrases

Use "How much" and comparatives:

Ask the price of a scarf: "___How much is this scarf?"
Compare two bags (one is cheaper): "___This bag is cheaper than that one."
Ask for a discount: "___Is there a discount if I buy two?"
Ask for the receipt: "___Could I have the receipt, please?"
Chapter 4: Getting There

Tânia at
Bella Souvenirs

A complete shopping experience

Artifact

The Receipt

Bella Souvenirs

Fine Handmade Gifts

Blue Silk Scarf€35.00€27.50
Red Wool Scarf€28.00€27.50
Subtotal€63.00
Discount-€8.00
Total€55.00
PaymentCredit Card
Thank you for shopping!
Dialogue

Shopping at Bella Souvenirs

T
Good afternoon! I am looking for souvenirs for my family.
S
Welcome! We have scarves, bags, and handmade jewelry. What are you looking for?
T
I love that blue scarf. How much is it?
S
That one is thirty-five euros.
T
And the red one? Is it cheaper?
S
The red one is twenty-eight euros. It is smaller, but very popular.
T
I would like both. Is there a discount if I buy two?
S
Yes! If you buy two, the total is fifty-five euros instead of sixty-three.
T
That is a good deal! Could I pay with a credit card?
S
Of course! Here is your receipt. Would you like a bag?
Comprehension

About the Dialogue

1. What does Tânia want to buy?
She wants to buy souvenirs — two scarves.
2. Which scarf is cheaper?
The red scarf is cheaper — twenty-eight euros.
3. How much does Tânia pay in total?
Fifty-five euros (with a discount of eight euros).
Listening

Shopping Conversation

Listen to Tânia shopping at Bella Souvenirs.

1. What does Tânia buy?
Two scarves — a blue one and a red one.
2. How much does she pay?
Fifty-five euros (with a discount).
Listening

Listen for Prices and Comparisons

Listen again. Count how many times Tânia uses "How much" or comparatives.

1. What price expressions does Tânia use?
"How much is it?" and "Is it cheaper?"
2. What comparative does the shopkeeper use?
"Smaller" — the red scarf is smaller but very popular.
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Fire

Click on each card to reveal the answer.

1. You see a nice bag. Ask the price.
"Excuse me, how much is this bag?"
2. You want a bigger size.
"Do you have this in a larger size?"
3. Ask if they accept credit cards.
"Do you accept credit cards?"
4. Ask for a discount.
"Is there a discount if I buy two?"
5. Compare two items.
"This scarf is cheaper than that one."
6. Ask for the receipt.
"Could I have the receipt, please?"
Detective

Spot the Error

"How much it costs?"
"How much does it cost?"
"This is more cheap than that."
"This is cheaper than that."
"I would like pay with card."
"I would like to pay with a card."
"Do you have this in big size?"
"Do you have this in a bigger size?"
"Could I has the receipt?"
"Could I have the receipt?"
0 / 5 errors found
Speaking

Oral Practice

1. Ask about the price of a bag.
"Excuse me, how much is this bag?"
2. Say you want to buy souvenirs for your grandchildren.
"I would like to buy some souvenirs for my grandchildren."
3. Compare two scarves.
"The blue scarf is more expensive than the red one."
4. Ask for a larger size.
"Do you have this in a larger size, please?"
5. Ask to pay with cash.
"Could I pay in cash?"
6. Ask for a discount.
"Is there a discount if I buy two?"
Build

Fix and Improve

Make these sentences correct:

"How much it costs?" ➔ "___How much does it cost?"
"This is more cheap." ➔ "___This is cheaper."
"I would like pay with card." ➔ "___I would like to pay with a card."
Compare

Lesson 5 vs Lesson 6

Lesson 5: Could I Have

Ordering food politely at a restaurant

"Could I have the grilled fish, please?"

Lesson 6: How Much + Comparatives

Asking prices and comparing items while shopping

"How much is this? This one is cheaper."

Mixed Practice

Restaurant or Shop?

You want to know the price of a bag: "___How much is this bag?"
You want the grilled fish at a restaurant: "___Could I have the grilled fish, please?"
Compare two scarves: "___This scarf is cheaper than that one."
Ask for the bill at a restaurant: "___Could I have the bill, please?"
Chapter 6: Your Turn

Time to Speak
